titleStmt.title: Temporal Switching to Extend the Bandwidth of Thin Absorbers

funder.orgName: Air Force Office of Scientific Research

analytic.title: Temporal Switching to Extend the Bandwidth of Thin Absorbers

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Wave absorption in time-invariant, passive thin films is fundamentally limited by a trade-off between bandwidth and overall thickness. In this work, we investigate the use of temporal switching to reduce signal reflections from a thin grounded slab over broader bandwidths. We extend quasi-normal mode theory to time switching, developing an ab initio formalism that can model a broad class of time-switched structures. Our formalism provides optimal switching strategies to maximize the bandwidth over which minimal reflection is achieved, showing promising prospects for time-switched nanophotonic and metamaterial systems to overcome the limits of time-invariant, passive structures. Time-varying media have been recently raising significant attention in the broad physics and engineering communities, given their opportunities in the context of magnet-free non-reciprocity [1]-[5], multifunctional metasurfaces [6], PT-symmetric structures [7]-[8], amplification [9] and symmetry breaking for emission and absorption [10]. Since the pioneering work of Morgenthaler [11], research on dynamic media has mainly focused on time-periodic systems [12]-[17], for which the analysis is facilitated by the Floquet theorem. Imparting arbitrary time modulation schemes,

beyond periodic, to wave-matter interactions can provide new possibilities and significantly broaden the field of dynamic media. Along this line, exotic wave phenomena have been recently shown in non-periodic time-varying structures, such as unlimited accumulation of energy [18]- [19] and arbitrary transport tuning via reconfigurable effective static potentials [20]. These studies have so far heavily relied on inverse engineering of the temporal modulation schemes, enabling only targeted functionalities and/or requiring simplifying assumptions like weak couplings. At the other extreme compared to periodically varying media, instantaneous temporal switching has drawn increasing attention as a platform for wave engineering [16], [21]- [22]. Abruptly changing in time the properties of an unbounded medium introduces a temporal boundary for wave propagation, dual of a spatial interface, generating forward and backward waves for which, instead of frequency, momentum is conserved and for which analytical solutions exist [22]. When considering more complicated structures, finite in one or more spatial dimensions and open to radiation, however, temporal switching interplays with other scattering phenomena and the analysis rapidly becomes very challenging in the absence of general constrains such as flux conservation.

To establish a thorough analysis of temporal switching in open systems, input-output techniques appear to be an ideal tool, since they can treat the field redistribution in a scatterer in time domain after the abrupt change of its properties as initial conditions to study the field evolution and associated scattering processes. This approach, initially established in quantum optics [24]- [25], has been applied to quantum networks [26]- [28], photon and charge transport [29]- [32] and quantum scattering [33]. As a variant for classical wave physics, coupled-mode theory (CMT) has been extensively used to model coupled resonator systems, well suited in regimes near resonance [34]- [36]. Except for few specific scenarios [37]- [38], its derivation follows from general principles, leaving many free phenomenological parameters undetermined. Nevertheless, there are two ab initio routes towards a rigorous input-output formalism. The first one is based on Feshbach's projector technique [39]- [40], based on the derivation of suitable system-bath Hamiltonians [33], [41]- [44], whereas the second one involves an expansion in terms of quasinormal modes (QNMs) [45]- [50]. The former, involving Hermitian system Hamiltonian, cannot easily incorporate material loss and dispersion, while the latter typically focuses on scattering in frequency domain and for convenience deals with background and scattered fields, and thus its connection with CMT is vague since, for example, the background fields cannot be identified as inputs in CMT [48].

The goal of this work is twofold: first we extend the QNM approach to time-switched open systems [48]- [49], developing a generalized temporal input-output theory that bridges the gap between QNMs and conventional CMT. This approach is ideally suited for time-switched open systems, and yields new physical insights into the exotic wave phenomena arising in them. We then apply this theory to the analysis of temporal switching in thin-layer absorbers, in order to overcome the trade-off between bandwidth and thickness that applies to any time-invariant passive absorber [23].

Thin absorbers and bandwidth limitations -Absorbing incident radiation over a large bandwidth is an important functionality for a variety of technologies, from radar technologies to anechoic measurements. Broadband absorption comes at the cost of thicker and heavier absorbing layers: consider for instance a Dallenbach screen, consisting of a homogeneous lossy nonmagnetic layer of thickness 𝑑 backed by a perfect electric conductor (PEC), as in Fig. 1a. For normal incidence, the complex reflection coefficient 𝜌 𝜔 = %&'( )*+,/. / 0* %&'( )*+,/. / 1* [51], where the refractive index 𝑛 = 𝜀 4 1 -𝑗𝜎/ 𝜀 : 𝜀 4 𝜔 is written in terms of the dielectric conductivity 𝜎 and the real-valued (dispersion-less) relative permittivity 𝜀 4 , and 𝑐 : = 1 𝜇 : 𝜖 : is the speed of light in vacuum, with µ : ϵ : being the permeability (permittivity). A thin screen with 𝜀 4 ≫ 1 optimally absorbs for

and 𝜎 ABC ≈ 2 𝜀 4 ABC tanh 0I 1 𝜀 4 ABC , where 𝜎 = σ𝜂 : 𝑑 with 𝜂 : = 𝜇 : /𝜖 : being the free-space impedance and 𝜆 : = 2π𝑐 : /𝜔 : the free-space wavelength at the operating frequency 𝜔 : , yielding 𝜌 𝜔 : = 0 . Correspondingly, the maximum bandwidth to least thickness ratio Δλ/d ≈ 32𝜌 : /𝜋 for this geometry [52], where 𝜌 : is the largest acceptable value of 𝜌 𝜔 within the operating bandwidth Δλ centered around 𝜆 : . This optimal condition falls within the more general Rozanov bound Δλ/d < 16/ lnρ : , valid for any nonmagnetic, passive, time-invariant absorber [23]. To reach this limit, further dispersion engineering of the material is generally required.

div.head: QNM formulation-
div.p: Given its open resonance, the response of the Dallenbach screen in Fig. 1(a) can be well captured by a QNM formulation. Assuming normal incidence along the positive 𝑧-
|Ψ AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 = 𝑎 𝑡 + 𝑧 : 𝑐 :
-1 1 + |Ψ j 𝑧 : , 𝑡 . (5) and ( 6), we get . /
div.formula: |𝛹 * 𝑧 = 𝑐 * sin 𝑘 * 𝑧 , -tan 𝑘 * 𝑧 : cos 𝑘 * 𝑧 • , 𝑧 : ≤ 𝑧 ≤ 0 -𝑐 * sin 𝑘 * 𝑧 : 𝑒 ) ‡ n ˆ/ Z0Z / -1, 1 • , 𝑧 ≤ 𝑧 : ,(3)
-j d dt 𝐴 * 𝑡 = 𝜔 * 𝐴 * 𝑡 + Ψ * * 𝑧 |𝑀|𝑠 1 𝑧, 𝑡 𝑑𝑧 : Z / .(5
div.p: [Eq. ( 6)]. We emphasize that the infinite summation in 𝐾 τ typically does not converge, but the observable quantities, such as 𝑏 AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 , do [33]. The ab initio input-output formulation Eq. ( 7) manifests causality, and can be applied beyond the weak-coupling limit, in contrast with the conventional CMT. To the best of our knowledge, Eq. ( 7) is the first example of rigorous inputoutput formalism based on QNMs, ideally suited to tackle time-switched open resonators as discussed later.

Our input-output formulation Eq. ( 7) collapses to conventional CMT in the limit in which the response is dominated by a single QNM-pair with complex-frequency 𝜔 ±I , such that the internal are normalized to the time-averaged power flux under the slowly-varying envelope approximation. The direct coupling coefficient C connecting the complex excitation with the outgoing fields is C ≈ e Gm+ / Z / /. / + 𝐾 0I τ 𝑒 0)¥+ / 𝑐 : /𝑧 : 𝑑τ : 0GZ / /. / , with C → 1 for our thin absorber in the limit 𝜀 4 ≫ 1. In Ref.
[57], we examine Eq. ( 8) analytically in the lossless scenario and compare it with the general principle formulation. As expected, Eq. ( 8)

converges to the conventional CMT [36] in the weak-coupling regime. Yet, when strong coupling and/or material dissipation is present, for which CMT cannot apply, our ab initio input-output formalism Eq. ( 7) provides a generalized tool to analytically study the scattering problem.
We apply our formalism to study the scattering from a time-switched Dallenbach screen. Consider,
where 𝜌 𝜔 is the complex reflection coefficient derived above, and the complex wave number
. We can employ Eq. ( 7) to evaluate the reflection 𝑏 AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 starting from an arbitrary time 𝑡 j , after knowing the initial condition 𝜓 * 𝑡 j = Ψ * * 𝑧 |𝑀|Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 j 𝑑𝑧 : Z /

. Note that 𝜓 * 0 = 0, since initially the incoming wave packet is far away from the screen, and 𝜓 * 𝑡 j , 𝑡 j > 0 can be calculated exactly from the knowledge of the internal field |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 j in Eq. ( 9) [58].
Consider the Dallenbach screen with 𝑑/𝜆 : = 0.03 and optimal material parameters 𝜀 4 ABC = 69.85
and 𝜎 ABC = 1.998. In Fig. 1 In the same subfigure, we also show the result from conventional CMT Eq. ( 8), which works well in the 𝜀 4 ≫ 1 regime except for an initial short period.

Time-switched absorber -Our ab initio formalism is ideally suited to study the effect of abruptly switching the properties of the Dallenbach screen. Specifically, we consider the case in which the relative permittivity abruptly changes at time 𝑡 j from 𝜀 I to 𝜀 G . The time-domain response of the screen after 𝑡 j cannot be deduced from Fourier analysis, but it can be readily obtained from our input-output formalism Eq. ( 7), once knowing the internal field |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 j 1 , 𝑧 : ≤ 𝑧 ≤ 0 immediately after the switch. To this end, we employ the continuity conditions for the electric displacement and magnetic induction across the temporal boundary at t OE [59] - [60], which equivalently reads |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 j 1 = 𝜀 I /𝜀 G 0 0 1 |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 j 0 . In turn, the internal field |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 j 0 immediately before the switch can be calculated from Eq. ( 9). Generally, the abrupt change of material properties does not preserve energy, and the injected energy ∆E ≈ 𝜓 0I 𝑡 j 1 G -𝜓 0I 𝑡 j 0 G using Eq. ( 8).

In Fig. 2, we study the effect of this abrupt switching on the reflection coefficient of the optimal absorber. In Fig. 2 being the total outgoing/incoming energy. The total reflected energy (upper orange surface) is larger than the optimal static absorber, i.e., the case 𝜀 G = 𝜀 I = 𝜀 4 ABC . However, the switching operation spreads the reflected energy over a wide range of frequencies outside the bandwidth of the incident pulse. The relative reflected energy, as well as the visibility of the target, is therefore reduced (lower green mesh surface) when we replace the total reflected energy 𝐸 AxC with the filtered energy 𝐸 pÃ AxC within the pulse frequency window 𝜔 : -∆𝜔, 𝜔 : + ∆𝜔 with ∆𝜔 = 𝛿𝜔 = 0.2𝜔 : , which covers 98% of the total incoming energy.

The switching mechanism effectively minimizes detectability in reflection by spreading the nonabsorbed energy over a broad frequency range, as explored in Refs. [61]- [65] for lossless periodically modulated screens. Our approach combines reduced reflection due to absorption with spreading arising through a single switching event, making the functionality efficient and effective.

We envision these devices being operated passively as a regular absorber using phase change materials, with its phase transition being triggered by the arrival of an incoming pulse to improve their bandwidth performance beyond the limits of passive absorbers.

For given switching time 𝑡 j , the filtered reflection energy 𝐸 pÃ AxC monotonically decreases with increasing switching strength 𝜀 G -𝜀 I when the initial permittivity 𝜀 I = 𝜀 4 ABC , until the system reaches the EP in Fig. 1(c), see Fig. For given switching amplitude, we find an optimal switching time 𝑡 j ABC that minimizes 𝐸 pÃ AxC . In suited to analyze temporally switched scenarios. This general formulation, derived from a QNM expansion, is exact and enables a clean first-principle derivation of conventional CMT. Using this approach, we found that temporal switching can effectively reduce the reflection of impinging pulses, and may strengthen the effectiveness of non-magnetic thin absorbers. Our approach can be extended to time switched metamaterials and metasurfaces, and scattering problems involving nanoparticles, for which the QNM expansion can be efficiently employed. identified to be simply 𝜓 0I 𝑡 since, in the weak coupling limit 𝜀 4 → ∞ and the lossless scenario 𝜎 = 0, its complex frequency 𝜔 0I approaches to the (positive) resonant frequency and the total stored energy inside the layer, i. , satisfying similarly 𝑃 AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 = 𝑏 1 AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 G . Finally, decoupling the positive frequency with the negative ones in Eq. ( 7) of the main text in the spirit of the rotating-wave approximation and employing the slow-envelope approximation for the complex excitation 𝑎 1 d* 𝑧 : , 𝑡 , we can reach the conventional CMT under one QNM-pair approximation, i.e., Eq. ( 8) of the main text.
Next, we consider lossless screens and analytically examine the derived conventional CMT.

We start our discussion by rewriting Eq. (8) [see main text] in the conventional form [68] where the resonant (angular) frequency of the single mode Ω = Re 𝜔 0I , its decay rate Γ = Im 𝜔 0I , the coupling between the mode and the port K = D = -𝑐 0I sin 𝑘 0I 𝑧 : + 𝑂 1/𝜀 4 in the weak-coupling limit when 𝜀 4 → ∞. We point out that in the process of decoupling the positive and negative frequencies for the derivation of the conventional CMT in Eq. (S10), we exclude the contribution of QNM n = 1

for the direct path C. This contribution is 𝐾 I τ 𝑒 0)¥+ / 𝑐 : /𝑧 : 𝑑τ : 0GZ / /. / , 𝜔 : = 𝜔 0I and supposed to be of higher order since the operation frequency 𝜔 : is far away from the complex frequency 𝜔 I of the QNM 𝑛 = 1. Indeed, in the scenario here, it approaches to 2 -16/𝜋 G /𝜀 4 when 𝜀 4 → ∞.
In the weak-coupling regime where 𝜀 4 → ∞ and when σ = 0 for the lossless screen, we can also expand the other system parameters in Eq. (S10). Specifically, we have
div.formula: K = D = j G. / 0Z / I ‰ v + I G‰ v Ú/• + 𝑂 I ‰ v Û/• and Γ = . / 0Z / ‰ v 1 + I Ü‰ v + 𝑂 I ‰ v •
div.p: . We can easily see that in the leading order the system parameters as derived satisfy all the requirements imposed by the timereversal symmetry and energy conservation principles, which are 𝐷 1 𝐷 = 2Γ, K = D and C𝐷 * = -𝐷 [68]. Finally, we remark that the performance of the conventional CMT as Eq. (S10) will not be improved when we incorporate higher-order terms in system parameters, since in its derivation the crucial (rotating-wave) approximation has been already made by decoupling the positive and negative frequencies. Typically, the approximated equation under the constraints of general principles will behave relatively better.

div.head: II. Formation of a QNM EP
div.p: We analyze the formation of the QNM EP y 𝜀 4 ¹º = -𝑗𝑝, p > 0 as seen in Fig. (S12) and (S13), which enables an analytical result for the expansion coefficient 𝑥 I :

(2), provide a relation for the expansion coefficients 𝐴 * 𝑡 = Ψ * * 𝑧 |𝑀|Ψ j 𝑧, 𝑡 𝑑𝑧 : Z / of the scattered field: We can then derive the outgoing field |Ψ AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 = 𝑏 𝑡 + formalism -Based on Eqs. (5) and (6), we can introduce an input-output formalism in terms of the modal coefficients 𝜓 * 𝑡 ≡ Ψ * * 𝑧 |𝑀|Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 𝑑𝑧 : Z / of |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 with respect to the QNMs. Specifically, using the expression 𝐴 * 𝑡 = 𝜓 * 𝑡 -Ψ * * 𝑧 |𝑀|Ψ p 𝑧, 𝑡 𝑑𝑧 : Z / in Eqs.

figure.figDesc: Fig. 1(a), a wave packet 𝑎 𝑡 -Z . / = 𝑓 𝑡 -Z0Z ./ cos 𝜔 : 𝑡 -Z0Z ./ with normalized envelope

(b), we show the reflection spectrum (green-solid line), together with the frequency spectrum 𝑎 𝜔 /𝑎 𝜔 : (red-dashed line) for an impinging pulse with FWHM 𝛿𝜔 = 0.2𝜔 : and initial position 𝑧 d = 200𝑧 : . In Fig. 1(c), we show the evolution of the first few (normalized) QNM frequencies 𝑦 * = ω ' z : ε ¸/c : , 𝑛 = ±1, ±2 as we vary 𝜀 4 from 140 to 2, with 𝜎 = 𝜎 ABC . The green plus and red cross symbols correspond respectively to 𝜀 4 = 𝜀 4 ABC for the optimal absorber and 𝜀 4 = 𝜀 4 ¹º ≈ 2.1 for the permittivity value corresponding to an exceptional point (EP) where the 𝑛 = ±1 QNMs coalesce [57]. Around the EP, the complex frequency y ±I 𝜀 4 of the 𝑛 = ±1 QNMs follow a square root behavior, a characteristic signature of secondorder EP singularities, as y ±I 𝜀 4 ≈ -j1.84 ± 1.53 𝜀 4 -𝜀 4 ¹º [57]. Using Eq. (7) and the calculated QNMs, we evaluate the temporal evolution 𝑏 AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 /𝑎 B©ªŽ of the slab reflection [green circles in Fig. 1(d)], which fits perfectly with the exact result provided by Eq. (9) [black solid line].

(a), we abruptly change 𝜀 4 at an arbitrary switching time 𝑡 j ∈ 45, 55 (in units of 1/𝜔 : ) from 𝜀 I = 𝜀 4 ABC to 𝜀 G ∈ 10, 120 . For each pair 𝑡 j , 𝜀 G , we calculate the relative total reflected energy 10log I: 𝐸 AxC /𝐸 d* , where 𝐸 AxC/d* = 𝑃 AxC/d* 𝑧 :

2(b) plotted for 𝑡 j = 49 1/𝜔 : . Around the EP, our inputoutput formalism converges increasingly slow, as also seen from the deteriorated comparison with conventional CMT Eq. (8) [blue dashed line]. In the left inset, we zoom around 𝜀 G ≈ 𝜀 4 ¹º , highlighting the rapid change in reflection, and thus the sensitivity around the EP, with a deterioration in overall response. In the right inset, we plot the normalized injected energy ∆E/𝐸 d* by switching as a function of 𝜀 G . Depending on whether ε G < 𝜀 I = 𝜀 4 ABC , we inject or extract increasing energy as the switching amplitude 1 2

figure.figDesc: Fig. 3, we fix ε G = 8, and calculate the effective reflection spectrum 𝜌 = 𝑏 AxC 𝜔 /𝑎 𝜔 for

figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. Performance of switched absorbers. (a) The upper orange surface presents the total

figure.head: Fig. 3 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3. The effective reflection spectrum ρ of the time-switched absorber for various impinging

1(c) of the main text. This EP arising as the merging of the n = ±1 QNMs is of the second order. Therefore, around the EP, we expand the complex frequency y ±I 𝜀 4 of the QNMs as y ±I 𝜀 4 = -jp ± 𝑥 I ∆𝜀 + 𝑥 G ∆𝜀 + ⋯ , ∆𝜀 = 𝜀 4 -𝜀 4 ¹º , and intend to determine the parameters p and 𝑥 I explicitly. To this end, we substitute this ansatz for y ±I 𝜀 4 into the transcendental equation tan 𝑘 * 𝑧 : = : 𝑘 * /𝜔 * where 𝑘 * = ‰ v . / 𝜔 * 1 -𝑗𝜎/ 𝜀 : 𝜀 4 𝜔 * for the complex frequencies 𝜔 * , or equivalently where 𝑦 * = 𝜔 * 𝑧 : 𝜀 4 /𝑐 : , 𝑧 : = -𝑑 and 𝜎 = σ𝑑 𝜇 : /𝜖 : . Then, expanding Eq. (S11) order by order with respect to ∆𝜀 and equating the resulting coefficient of each power of ∆𝜀 to zero, we obtain up to O ∆𝜀 the EP requires that 𝑥 I ≠ 0, and then combining Eqs. (S12) and (S13) allows us to evaluate the EP y 𝜀 4 ¹º = -𝑗𝑝 with In turn, with a given 𝜎 value for the normalized conductivity, substituting the expression for p in Eq. (S14) into Eq. (S12) allows us to determine the parameter 𝜀 4 ¹º . For example, in the case of the main text when 𝜎 = 𝜎 ABC = 1.998, we get 𝜀 4 ¹º ≈ 2.1 and correspondingly p ≈ 1.84 using Eq. (S14). To obtain the expansion coefficient 𝑥 I of y ±I 𝜀 4 around the EP with respect to ∆𝜀, we need to resort to the next order O ∆𝜀 , which after considering Eqs. (S12) and (S13) can be simplified to be Interestingly, the expansion coefficient 𝑥 G is absence in Eq. (S15) due to the relationships in Eqs.

field |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 inside the layer as the superposition |Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 = |Ψ p 𝑧, 𝑡 + |Ψ j 𝑧, 𝑡 , where the

|Ψ 𝑧, 𝑡 = Our QNMs constitute a complete basis [55]-[56], and satisfy the orthonormality condition 𝐸 ὴ: consists of the electric and magnetic field components 𝐸 ` and 𝐻 b within the 𝐻 b layer -d = 𝑧 : < 𝑧 < 0 . condition for Eq. (1) at all instants t.

have the power-normalized complex amplitude 𝑏 1 AxC 𝑧 : , 𝑡 = 2/𝜂 :

figure.figDesc: . In the scenario when σ = 0 as focused here, we have the following relationships 𝑘 * 𝑧 : = 𝑦 * , tan 𝑦 * = -𝑗 𝜀 4 and 𝑐 * G 𝜀 : 𝑧 : = 1/𝜀 4 . In addition, the (normalized) complex frequency 𝑦 * ≡ 𝜔 * 𝑧 : 𝜀 4 /𝑐 : of QNMs can be explicitly given as -𝑦 0* * = 𝑦 * =

QNM for the direct path (see the main text) can be simplified greatly to be 𝐾 * 𝜏 =
𝜏 . Therefore, we can easily calculate the (approximated) direct path C in Eq.
(S10), which turns out to be C = 1 + j

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: -This work was supported by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research and the Simons Foundation.

div.head: Temporal Switching to Extend the Bandwidth of Thin Absorbers
div.p: Huanan Li, 1 and Andrea Alù

div.head: I. First-principle derivation of conventional coupled-mode theory (CMT)
div.p: In this section, we present a first-principle derivation of the conventional CMT using the inputoutput formulation Eq. ( 7) of the main text. We first note that the QNMs appear in pairs [66] and . This approximation is valid for our thin absorber where 𝜀 4 ≫ 1 and when the operating frequency 𝜔 : ~Re 𝜔 0I > 0. As we know, the conventional CMT deals with the energy-normalized positive-frequency component of the mode amplitude [34]. Here, it can be

Parametric amplification and bidirectional invisibility in PT-symmetric time-Floquet systems
Nonreciprocal gain in non-hermitian time-Floquet systems
Breaking temporal symmetries for emission and absorption
Velocity Modulation of Electromagnetic Waves
Parametric properties of fields in a slab of time-varying permittivity

Virtual perfect absorption through modulation of the radiative decay rate
Driving-induced metamorphosis of transport in arrays of coupled resonators

Cavity-based quantum networks with single atoms and optical photons

Quantum dynamics of propagating photons with strong interactions: a generalized input-output formalism
Generalized input-output method to quantum transport junctions. I. General formulation
Generalized input-output method to quantum transport junctions. II. Applications
Ab initio few-mode theory for quantum potential scattering problems
Temporal coupled-mode theory for the fano resonance in optical resonators
Temporal coupled-mode theory and the presence of nonorthogonal modes in lossless multimode cavities
Active coupled-resonator optical waveguides. I. Gain enhancement and noise
Virtual Parity-Time Symmetry
Unified theory of nuclear reactions
A unified theory of nuclear reactions. II
Field quantization for open optical cavities
Quantum theory of multimode fields: applications to optical resonators

Projection-operator approach to potential scattering
Quantized mode of a leaky cavity
Quasimodal expansion of electromagnetic fields in open two-dimensional structures
Resonant-state expansion of dispersive open optical systems: creating gold from sand
Light Interaction with Photonic and Plasmonic Resonances
Rigorous modal analysis of plasmonic nanoresonators
Quantization of quasinormal modes for open cavities and plasmonic cavity quantum electrodynamics

monogr.title: Elementary Applied Partial Differential Equations with Fourier Series and Boundary Value Problems

As a comparison, for a valid QNM expansion |Ψ z, t = ψ ' t ' |Ψ ' z of the state vector |Ψ z, t , a seemingly similar equality H|Ψ z, t = ψ ' t ' ω ' |Ψ ' z does not hold, which hinders a construction of an
